I failed an Angular interview because of one RxJS question.
Not a hard question. A basic one. The interviewer asked why my search box was firing five API calls for one word, and I had no real answer beyond “that is just how it works.”
It was not how it works. It was how I had written it, wrong, for two years, without anyone ever checking.
I went home that night and actually read the RxJS documentation instead of copying patterns from old projects. Four patterns changed everything. Not forty. Four.
Here they are, with the exact numbers that finally made them click for me.
Pattern 1: combineLatest for Streams That Depend on Each Other
The problem
A filter screen with two dropdowns, category and sort order, each one a separate observable. The junior instinct is to subscribe to both separately and manage the combination by hand.
export class ProductListComponent implements OnInit {
products: Product[] = [];
private category = 'all';
private sort = 'price'; ngOnInit() {
this.categoryChange$.subscribe(cat => {
this.category = cat…
